data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "A trans-regional bronze procurement crisis severed the military-ritual supply chains that sustained palace legitimacy","mechanism": "The Cypriot copper-tin alloy trade collapsed due to combined Tin Route disruption (Alpine/Iberian exhaustion) and maritime piracy along Syrian entrepôt routes, triggering a metallic-currency liquidity crisis in palace economies where bronze weapon-allotment constituted warrior wages and votive bronze consumption underwrote priestly redistribution; palace centers could neither arm retainers nor stage temple ceremonies, so aristocracies fragmented into self-provisioning local strongholds abandoning administrative palatial tiers","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece","Hittite Anatolia","Ugarit","Levantine city-states"],"excludes": ["Egypt","Mesopotamian polities","non-palatial Iron Age successors"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Tin ingot stockpiles found in palatial storerooms dating 1180-1150 BC comparable to pre-crisis levels","eval": "rubric:std-hist"},{"case": "Archaeological evidence of large-scale palace bronze-working continuing without tin-source substitution through 1150 BC","eval": "rubric:std-hist"},{"case": "Linear B and Hittite texts recording continued foreign bronze deliveries or only mild tin-price inflation 1225-1180 BC","eval": "rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes": "Emphasizes supply-chain vulnerability of bronze-dependent prestige economies; Egypt excluded due to its gold-silver bimetallic temple economy and local copper sources"}
